title: Dog Scratching Ear Until Bleeding: Causes, Triage & Next Steps
meta_desc: If your dog is scratching their ear until it bleeds, they may have an infection or mites. Learn about urgency levels and how to provide relief.
content_html:
What is happening to your dog?

When a dog scratches their ear to the point of bleeding, it typically indicates intense itching, known as pruritus, or significant pain. This cycle of scratching often leads to self-trauma, resulting in skin tears, scabs, or even an aural hematoma (a blood-filled pocket in the ear flap). The root cause is frequently an underlying issue such as a bacterial or yeast infection, ear mites, environmental allergies, or a foreign body like a grass seed lodged in the canal.

Is this an emergency?

The urgency level for a dog scratching until they bleed is Medium. While it is not typically a sudden life-threatening emergency, it represents a high level of physical distress for your pet. You should schedule a veterinary appointment within the next 24 to 48 hours to prevent the condition from worsening. Immediate attention is required if your dog is also tilting their head significantly, losing balance, or if the bleeding is heavy and difficult to stop.

How a photo helps triage

Providing a clear, close-up photo of the affected ear can be incredibly helpful for a veterinary triage team. A photo allows professionals to see the color and consistency of any discharge, the degree of inflammation, and the specific location of the bleeding. This information helps us determine if the issue is a superficial skin infection or a deeper canal problem, ensuring your pet receives the appropriate priority for an appointment.

faqs.question: Can I put peroxide or alcohol in my dog's ear to clean the blood?
faqs.answer: No, you should avoid using harsh chemicals like peroxide or alcohol. These can be extremely painful on open wounds and can cause permanent damage or deafness if the eardrum is ruptured.

faqs.question: How can I stop my dog from scratching right now?
faqs.answer: The most effective temporary solution is to place an Elizabethan collar (the 'cone of shame') on your dog. This prevents their paws from reaching the ear and causing further injury while you wait for your vet visit.

faqs.question: What if my dog's ear flap looks swollen like a balloon?
faqs.answer: This is likely an aural hematoma, caused by broken blood vessels from vigorous shaking or scratching. This requires veterinary intervention to drain the fluid and address the underlying cause of the irritation.
